title: Medicine on the Edge: Comparative Performance Analysis of On-Device LLMs for Clinical Reasoning
authors: Leon NissenStanford Mussallem Center for Biodesign, Stanford University, 318 Pasteur Drive, Stanford, California, USAInstitute for Digital Medicine, University Hospital Bonn, Venusberg-Campus 1, GermanyCorresponding author.leon.nissen@stanford.eduPhilip ZagarStanford Mussallem Center for Biodesign, Stanford University, 318 Pasteur Drive, Stanford, California, USAVishnu RaviStanford Mussallem Center for Biodesign, Stanford University, 318 Pasteur Drive, Stanford, California, USAAydin ZahedivashStanford Mussallem Center for Biodesign, Stanford University, 318 Pasteur Drive, Stanford, California, USALara Marie ReimerInstitute for Digital Medicine, University Hospital Bonn, Venusberg-Campus 1, GermanyStephan JonasInstitute for Digital Medicine, University Hospital Bonn, Venusberg-Campus 1, GermanyOliver AalamiStanford Mussallem Center for Biodesign, Stanford University, 318 Pasteur Drive, Stanford, California, USAPaul SchmiedmayerStanford Mussallem Center for Biodesign, Stanford University, 318 Pasteur Drive, Stanford, California, USA
abstract: The deployment of on mobile devices offers significant potential for medical applications, enhancing privacy, security, and cost-efficiency by eliminating reliance on cloud-based services and keeping sensitive health data local. However, the performance and accuracy of on-device in real-world medical contexts remain underexplored. In this study, we benchmark publicly available on-device using the AMEGA dataset, evaluating accuracy, computational efficiency, and thermal limitation across various mobile devices. Our results indicate that compact general-purpose models like Phi-3 Mini achieve a strong balance between speed and accuracy, while medically fine-tuned models such as Med42 and Aloe attain the highest accuracy. Notably, deploying on older devices remains feasible, with memory constraints posing a greater challenge than raw processing power. Our study underscores the potential of on-device for healthcare while emphasizing the need for more efficient inference and models tailored to real-world clinical reasoning.
